one_line_takeaway: MPU lets a server publish noised, symmetry-reparameterized copies of its model for client-side unlearning and then cancel the noise via harmonic aggregation, achieving unlearning quality close to (and sometimes exceeding) a fully-disclosed noise-free baseline while keeping both the server's parameters and the client's forget set undisclosed, with NP-hardness of copy alignment underpinning a computational-DP-style privacy argument.
key_finding: On the Llama-3.2-1B/TOFU headline table, MPU with m=2 and kappa=0.01 matches or exceeds CLEAN forget quality while clearly beating the NOISED (no-denoise) baseline: GradDiff FQ 0.405 (MPU) vs 0.266 (NOISED) vs 0.405 (CLEAN); NPO FQ 0.919 (MPU) vs 0.766 (NOISED) vs 0.919 (CLEAN); GradAscent FQ 5.41e-2 (MPU) vs 2.81e-8 (NOISED) vs 6.58e-5 (CLEAN); SimNPO FQ 9.71e-2 (MPU) vs 5.41e-2 (NOISED) vs 5.41e-2 (CLEAN); DPO FQ 0.266 (MPU) vs 0.165 for both NOISED and CLEAN. Model Utility varied by less than 0.01 across all three settings for every algorithm, indicating utility preservation is largely insensitive to the noise/denoise mechanism in this setup. Forget Truth Ratio and PrivLeak showed no significant differences across settings except for GradAscent, which the authors note exhibits a 'complete breakdown' (near-zero FQ and MU) under all settings including CLEAN. The paper reports that the server-side computational overhead is negligible: arithmetic intensity is about 4% of an A100's compute-bound ridge point, and estimated server latency Tserver is about 0.13 seconds per round on an A100, with memory footprint reduced from O(md) to O(d) via a streaming accumulator implementation (Section 3.5) that avoids holding all m model copies/updates simultaneously.
core_contribution: The paper proposes MPU (Multiple Perturbed Copies Unlearning), a server-client machine unlearning framework for LLMs that satisfies a 'dual non-disclosure' constraint: the client's forget set never leaves the client, and the server never exposes its exact model parameters. In each communication round the server (i) generates m>=2 perturbed copies of the current model by adding structured, block-wise zero-sum Gaussian noise and applying an invertible, function-preserving reparameterization (parameter-symmetry transform, including a RoPE-aware variant for Llama-style attention and MLP channel permutations for feed-forward blocks) sampled per-copy from a secret seed; (ii) the client runs a standard local unlearning algorithm (e.g., GradAscent, NPO, DPO) on each received copy against its private forget set and returns the resulting parameter updates; (iii) the server inverts the reparameterizations and aggregates the m returned updates with harmonic weights (proportional to the inverse of each copy's noise scaling factor alpha_k), which the paper proves algebraically cancels the first-order noise term because the injected noises are constructed to sum to zero across copies. The authors also prove that inverting the reparameterization (i.e., aligning copies to recover the original parameters) is equivalent to the Multi-Dimensional Assignment Problem for FFN permutations and the Generalized Orthogonal Procrustes Problem for attention symmetries, both NP-hard for m>=3, and use this hardness to argue a Computational Differential Privacy guarantee for the server's published copies without the usual DP privacy-utility tradeoff, since the zero-sum noise is provably cancelable by the (secret-key-holding) server but not by a computationally bounded client/adversary.
threat_model: Two mutually distrusting parties in a server-client unlearning protocol. The server holds a proprietary, fine-tuned LLM and does not want to reveal its exact current parameters to the client; the client holds a private 'forget set' (data whose influence must be removed from the model) and does not want to share raw data or sufficient statistics with the server. The adversary of interest is the client (or an eavesdropper on the published model copies) attempting to reconstruct the server's true parameters from the m perturbed, reparameterized copies it receives; the paper models this adversary as computationally bounded (probabilistic polynomial-time), which is the basis for the NP-hardness-based Computational DP argument in Appendix A.2. The paper does not model a malicious/Byzantine client that could send back manipulated updates, nor an adversary targeting the client's forget-set privacy beyond noting that client-side DP-SGD can be layered on top.
limitations: The paper's own conclusion states two copies (m=2) sufficed in all their experiments but that local client-side computation grows linearly with the number of copies m, and calls for future work on improved communication protocols and further cost reduction. The Computational DP argument (Appendix A.2) depends on the adversary being unable to solve an NP-hard worst-case alignment problem; the paper does not analyze average-case hardness or empirically attempt to break the reparameterization, so the practical security margin against a resourceful or structurally-informed adversary is asserted theoretically rather than demonstrated. The headline experimental comparison (Table 1) is reported for a single model (Llama-3.2-1B) on a single benchmark split (TOFU Split99) at a single noise level (kappa=0.01) with m=2; broader results across model sizes, kappa, and m are deferred to appendix tables not captured in the main-text extraction reviewed here. The threat model assumes a semi-honest/PPT client and does not address a malicious client that manipulates or withholds returned updates, nor collusion among multiple clients across rounds.

datasets_used: TOFU (Task of Fictitious Unlearning) benchmark, Split99
quantitative_results: GradDiff Forget Quality: CLEAN 0.405, NOISED 0.266, MPU 0.405
NPO Forget Quality: CLEAN 0.919, NOISED 0.766, MPU 0.919
GradAscent Forget Quality: CLEAN 6.58e-5, NOISED 2.81e-8, MPU 5.41e-2
SimNPO Forget Quality: CLEAN 5.41e-2, NOISED 5.41e-2, MPU 9.71e-2
DPO Forget Quality: CLEAN 0.165, NOISED 0.165, MPU 0.266
UnDIAL Forget Quality: CLEAN/NOISED/MPU all 1.43e-2 (no change)
SatImp Forget Quality: CLEAN 3.02e-3, NOISED 6.76e-3, MPU 6.76e-3
Model Utility variation across CLEAN/NOISED/MPU settings below 0.01 for all seven algorithms
Arithmetic intensity of MPU overhead: ~4% of A100 GPU's compute-bound ridge point
Estimated server-side latency Tserver: ~0.13 seconds per round on an A100 GPU

Copy-alignment (reparameterization-breaking) hardness: O(d^3) for m=2, NP-hard for m>=3 (Multi-Dimensional Assignment Problem for FFN permutations; Generalized Orthogonal Procrustes Problem for attention symmetries)
baselines_compared: CLEAN: noise-free, centralized single-copy unlearning with full model/data access (approximate upper bound)
NOISED: single-copy noise injection with no denoising (lower-bound ablation isolating the effect of harmonic denoising)
Seven underlying unlearning algorithms used as plug-in objectives: GradAscent, GradDiff, DPO, NPO, SimNPO, UnDIAL, SatImp
